What is a Modality?
Modality: the way information is expressed or perceived, e.g., language, vision, sound, touch.
From a sensor: raw modality is closest to the physical signal, abstract modality is derived.
The same phenomenon can appear across multiple modalities (e.g., emotion in voice and 
face)

Heterogeneous Modalities
Information present in different modalities will often show diverse qualities, structures 
and representations.

Dimensions of Heterogeneity

1. Structure

Static, temporal, spatial, or

hierarchical.

4. Granularity

Mismatched sampling rates and

resolutions.

2.Repr. Space

Discrete tokens vs. Continuous

embeddings.

5. Noise

Uncertainty and modality-

specific noise.

VN:UK

Institute for Research
& Exec

Structure
Static: single images, fixed embeddings.
Temporal: speech, videos, sequential sensor data.
Spatial: 2D images, 3D point clouds, spatial grids.
Hierarchical: document → sentence → word, scene → object → part.

Representation Space
Discrete: tokens, symbolic labels, graph nodes.
Continuous: embedding vectors, feature maps, latent states.
Interpretable vs. latent features (explicit scores vs. black-box features).

Information
Entropy: how uncertain or variable a modality is.
Density: information per unit (per frame, per second, per token).
Overlap and range: shared vs. unique information, and what each modality can 
represent.

Granularity
Different sampling rates: audio (kHz), video (fps), text (tokens/s).
Resolution: high-res images vs. coarse sensors; precision levels.
Granularity mismatch complicates temporal/spatial alignment and fusion.

Noise
Each modality has its own noise distribution and SNR.

Relevance
Not all modalities are equally useful for every task → The importance of a 
modality is context-dependent.
Emotion Detection: Prosody and facial expressions dominate.
Night Driving: Lidar/Radar are more relevant than RGB cameras.
→ Dynamic Weighting: Modern models use gating or attention m

Cross-Modal Interactions
Redundancy: multiple modalities carry the same signal (speech + gesture).
Dominance: one modality overrides another when they conflict.
Modulation and emergence: interpretation or new meaning depends on 
combining modalities.

Multimodal Pipeline
Preprocess each modality separately (normalization, tokenization, feature 
extraction).
Encode each modality into latent representations.
Fuse or interact representations, then apply task-specific heads for prediction or 
generation.

Input Module
Modality-Specific Encoders
Text: tokenization → embeddings → transformer encoder.
Image: CNN or Vision Transformer extracting spatial features.
Audio/sensors: temporal models over spectrograms or numerical features.

Fusion Module
The "Heart" of the system where individual data streams are merged.

Early Fusion (Feature-level)
Mechanism: Raw features are combined at the 
beginning of the network.
Pros: Captures high-level interactions between 
modalities early on, simple and parameter-efficient
Cons: Can struggle with strong heterogeneity and 
misalignment (e.g. 60fps videos and 44kHz audio),

Late Fusion (Decision-level)
Mechanism: Each modality is processed by an 
independent model. Only the final 
scores/predictions are combined (e.g., via 
averaging or voting or using a meta-learner).
Pros: Robust; if one modality is noisy (e.g., a blurry 
image), the other (e.g., clear audio) can sti

Intermediate Fusion (Feature-Level)
Intermediate fusion encodes modalities separately but merges their internal "hidden" 
representations (feature vectors) before the final decision-making layer. 
Separate encoders; interaction via cross-attention or shared transformer blocks.
Supports fine-grained

Gated Multimodal Fusion
Uses a "gate" (similar to an LSTM or GRU) to decide how much influence each modality should 
have on the final feature representation based on the input context.
Pros:
- Dynamic Weighting: The model can decide to "listen" more to the audio if the image is blurry, 
or "look" m

other.
